coredata.dc:title: Two new ternary Mn(II) coordination polymers by regulation of aromatic carboxylate ligands: Synthesis, structures, photocatalytic and selective ion-sensing properties
coredata.dc:creator: Xiao, Qing-Qing
Liu, Dong
Wei, Ying-Li
Cui, Guang-Hua
coredata.dc:description: Two new Mn(II) coordination polymers, namely, [Mn(NDC)(L)(H2O)] n (NCST-3) and {[Mn2(HBTC)2(L)2(H2O)2]·2H2O} n (NCST-4) (L = 1,4-bis(5,6-dimethylbenzimidazole)butane, 1,4-H2NDC = 1,4-naphthalene dicarboxylic acid, and H3BTC = 1,3,5-benzenetricarboxylic acid) (NCST = North China University of Science and Technology) were synthesized and structurally characterized by single crystal and powder X-ray diffractions, elemental analysis, FT-IR spectroscopy and TGA. NCST-3 exhibits 2D 3,5L2 network, whereas NCST-4 possesses an unusual (3,4,5)-connected framework defined by the point symbol of (53)(55.6)(56.62.82). Furthermore, the photocatalytic activities of two complexes were evaluated for the degradation of methylene blue (MB), Rhodamine B (RhB), methyl violet (MV) and methyl orange (MO) under the UV irradiation. Finally, two compounds as probes displayed favorable ion-sensing performance for Cr3+/Ag+ through fluorescence quenching.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mn(II) coordination polymers
Synthesis
Structures
Fluorescence quenching
Selective sensing
Inorganic ions

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Coordination environment around the Mn(II) in NCST-3 with 30% ellipsoids (symmetry codes: A = x, y-1, z; B = -x, y-1, -z+1; C = x-0.5, -y+0.5, z+0.5.); (b) View of the 2D layer of NCST-3; (c) 3,5L2 topology network of NCST-3; (d) 3D supramolecular architecture formed by CH···π stacking interactions in NCST-3.

(a) Coordination environment around the Mn(II) in NCST-4 with 30% ellipsoids (symmetry codes: A = x, y-1, z; B = -x, y-0.5, -z; C = -x+1, y-0.5, -z+1.); (b) View of the 2D layer of NCST-4; (c) (3,4,5)-connected new topology network of NCST-4; (d) 3D supramolecular architecture formed by OH⋯O stacking interactions in NCST-4.
(a) Fluorescence spectra of NCST-3 in various solvents; (b) Fluorescence spectra of NCST-4 in various solvents.
(a) Quenching percentage of NCST-3 in EtOH containing different cations; (b) Quenching percentage of NCST-4 in DMAC containing different cations.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of NCST-3 in different concentrations of Cr3+ ethanol solution; (b) Relationship between F 0/F and the concentration of Cr3+ ion (Inset: Stern-Volmer plot of NCST-3 for Cr3+ at the concentration of 0–50 μM).

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of NCST-4 in different concentrations of Ag+ DMAC solution; (b) Relationship between F 0/F and the concentration of Ag+ ion (Inset: Stern-Volmer plot of NCST-4 for Ag+ at the concentration of 0–100 μM).

(a) Fluorescence intensity of NCST-3 in the presence of different ions in EtOH without and with Cr3+; (b) Fluorescence intensity of NCST-4 in the presence of different ions in DMAC without and with Ag+.
Crystallographic data and refinement parameter for NCST-3 and NCST-4.
The selected bond lengths (Å) and angles (°) of NCST-3 and NCST-4.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Symmetry codes for NCST-3: A = x, y-1, z; B = -x, y-1, -z+1; C = x-0.5, -y+0.5, z+0.5. for NCST-4: A = x, y-1, z; B = -x, y-0.5, -z; C = -x+1, y-0.5, -z+1.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of luminescent property for sensing metal ions.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Where PIN/CdS = polyindole/cadmium sulphide, H2bpydb = 4,4′-(4,4′-bipyridine-2,6-diyl) dibenzoic acid, L1 = 1-substitued-2-carbazoleylbenzoimidazole, L2 = phenazine derivative based on 2,3-diamino-phenazine and 5-(4-chlorphenyl)-2-furaldehyde, L3 = 1-(4-(pyridine-4-yl)benzylidene)thiosemicarbazide.

head.ce:title: Two new ternary Mn(II) coordination polymers by regulation of aromatic carboxylate ligands: Synthesis, structures, photocatalytic and selective ion-sensing properties

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical Engineering, Hebei Key Laboratory for Environment Photocatalytic and Electrocatalytic Materials, North China University of Science and Technology, No. 21 Bohai Road, Caofeidian new-city, Tangshan, Hebei, 063210, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hebei Key Laboratory for Environment Photocatalytic and Electrocatalytic Materials
North China University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Two new Mn(II) coordination polymers, namely, [Mn(NDC)(L)(H2O)] n (NCST-3) and {[Mn2(HBTC)2(L)2(H2O)2]·2H2O} n (NCST-4) (L = 1,4-bis(5,6-dimethylbenzimidazole)butane, 1,4-H2NDC = 1,4-naphthalene dicarboxylic acid, and H3BTC = 1,3,5-benzenetricarboxylic acid) (NCST = North China University of Science and Technology) were synthesized and structurally characterized by single crystal and powder X-ray diffractions, elemental analysis, FT-IR spectroscopy and TGA. NCST-3 exhibits 2D 3,5L2 network, whereas NCST-4 possesses an unusual (3,4,5)-connected framework defined by the point symbol of (53)(55.6)(56.62.82). Furthermore, the photocatalytic activities of two complexes were evaluated for the degradation of methylene blue (MB), Rhodamine B (RhB), methyl violet (MV) and methyl orange (MO) under the UV irradiation. Finally, two compounds as probes displayed favorable ion-sensing performance for Cr3+/Ag+ through fluorescence quenching.

Two ternary Mn(II) coordination polymers NCST-3 and NCST-4 were synthesized by hydrothermal method. They exhibit 2D 3,5L2 network, and unusual (3,4,5)-connected framework, respectively. Further, NCST-3, as catalyst, presents higher photocatalytic activities than NCST-4 for the degradation of four organic dyes (MB, RhB, MV, and MO) under UV irradiation. Finally, NCST-3 and NCST-4 as fluorescence probes display high sensitivity and selectivity for the detection of Cr3+/Ag+.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Coordination polymers (CPs), as one of crystalline materials constructed by metal ions and organic ligands such as nitrogen-containing and oxygen-containing linkers, captured remarkable attention due to many potential applications including gas adsorption/separations, photocatalysis, electrocatalysis, and selective fluorescence recognition of diverse analytes [1–4]. In especial, CPs with large surface areas, tunable pore structure, and higher stability, are applied as photocatalysts for the degradation of organic dyes in wastewater [5–7]. Further, these coordination compounds can serve as fluorescent probe to distinguish harmful species such as ions, small organic molecules, owing to their high efficiency, selectivity, sensitivity and anti-interference [8–11]. Flexible bis(benzimidazole) derivatives have drawn much attention in recent years for the construction of novel CPs with diverse structures and properties because they can freely rotate to meet the coordination requirements of the metal centers [12,13]. Nevertheless, a few Mn(II) coordination polymers based on flexible bis(benzimidazole) and carboxylic acid co-ligands were previously reported [14–18]. As an example, our group synthesized and reported a Mn(II) coordination polymer with a (4,5)-connected xah network, which can serve as catalyst to degrade Congo red dye in a photo-Fenton-like process with degrading efficiency of 85% [19]. However, the fluorescence sensing properties of these Mn(II) CPs have been failed to consider. Acting as fluorescence probes to detect inorganic ions will open a new window for the application of Mn(II)-bis(benzimidazole) CPs.

Nowadays, inorganic ions, such as Cr3+, and Ag+, have an overwhelmingly negative influence on the environment even human health. Chromium ion (Cr3+) with toxicity and bioaccumulation is widely applied in industry such as leather tanning, electroplating, and printing and dying [20–24]. Moreover, Ag+, as a type of noble metal ion, possesses many applications in electrical, and photography/imaging industries. High concentration silver ions can disturb the biochemical processes through the inactivation of enzyme/protein or the imbalance of immunoregulatory [25,26]. The emission of silver-containing wastewater is one significant issue and monitoring the quantity of Ag+ is another problem [27,28]. There are many ways to detect these pollutants, but their applications are limited due to drawbacks, for example, low portability, complex pretreatments and expensive instruments. The luminescence sensing approaches by CPs have attracted much attention owing to nondestructive detection, high sensitivity, fast response time and real-time monitoring both in the environment and in organisms.

Herein, two CPs [Mn(NDC)(L)(H2O)] n (NCST-3) and {[Mn2(HBTC)2(L)2(H2O)2]·2H2O} n (NCST-4) (NCST = North China University of Science and Technology) were synthesized by the hydrothermal method (L = 1,4-bis(5,6-dimethylbenzimidazole)butane, 1,4-H2NDC = 1,4-naphthalene dicarboxylic acid, and H3BTC = 1,3,5-benzenetricarboxylic acid). Furthermore, NCST-3 and NCST-4 reveal selective fluorescent sensing properties for Cr3+, and Ag+, severally.

All of chemical reagents were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co, Ltd and employed without purification. Carbon, hydrogen, and nitrogen elemental analyses were carried out on a PerkinElmer 240C elemental analyzer. FT-IR spectra of NCST-3 and NCST-4 were measured as KBr pellets on a Nicolet Avatar 360 Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometer in the region of 4000 ‒ 400 cm−1. Thermal stabilities of these two coordination polymers from room temperature to 800 °C were investigated by Netzsch STA449 F1 differential thermal analyzer. Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) data were collected by a Rigaku D/Max-2500 diffractometer (Cu-Kα, λ = 1.5418 Å) from 5 to 50° with a step size of 0.02° in 2θ. Ultrasonic generation was performed in a KQ-300DE ultrasonic bath with the frequency of 40 kHz. The emission and excitation spectra were measured by an Edinburgh FS5 fluorescence spectrometer. The solid-state ultraviolet–visible adsorption spectra were collected by a Puxi T9 ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer with a standard BaSO4 plate at the room temperature. Photocatalytic experiments were conducted through a XPA-VII photochemical reactor (Xujiang, Nanjing) equipped with a high-pressure mercury lamp (500 W) as light source.

A mixture of L (34.6 mg, 0.1 mmol), Mn(OAc)2·4H2O (49.2 mg, 0.2 mmol), 1,4-H2NDC (43.2 mg, 0.2 mmol), and H2O (10.0 mL) was sealed in a 25.0 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel vessel, which was then heated to 140 °C within 10 h and kept at 140 °C for 72 h followed by slowly cooled to 25 °C within 24 h. Colorless blocked crystals of NCST-3 were collected for single crystal X-ray diffraction analysis. Yield: 38.4% based on L. Anal. Calc. for C34H34MnN4O5 (Fw = 633.59): C, 64.45; H, 5.41; N, 8.84%. Found: C, 64.58; H, 5.67; N, 8.61%. IR (KBr, cm−1): 3427s, 3111w, 2942w, 1573s, 1508 m, 1406s,1363s, 1209w, 1078 m, 802 m.

Colorless blocked crystals of NCST-4 were synthesized by the similar method except that 1,4-H2NDC was replaced by H3BTC. Yield: 52.6% based on L. Anal. Calc. for C62H68Mn2N8O16 (Fw = 1291.12): C, 57.68; H, 5.31; N, 8.68%. Found: C, 57.42; H, 5.53; N, 8.59%. IR (KBr, cm−1): 3446s, 3115w, 2942w, 1704s, 1613s, 1562s, 1506 m, 1445s, 1376s, 1274w, 1078 m, 848w, 684w.

Data collections were recorded at 296(2) K on a Bruker SMART 1000 CCD single-crystal X-ray diffractometer with graphite-monochromated Mo-Kα radiation (λ = 0.71073 Å) by the SMART and SAINT programs [29]. The empirical absorption corrections were applied by the SADABS program [30]. Olex2 software with the SHELXT-2014 was applied to solve the structures, which were further refined by SHELXL-2016 on the basis of F 2 with full-matrix least-squares and anisotropic thermal parameters for all non-hydrogen atoms [31]. Further, the H atoms of water were located from difference Fourier maps, and all of the others were calculated according to riding model (idealized geometry) [32]. Tables 1 and 2
show crystallographic data and details about the data collection, and selected bond lengths/angles, respectively.

The fluorescence sensing experiments are as follow: 4 mg of powder are added into glass vial (4 mL) containing 4 mL of 5 × 10−3 M different M(NO3) x solutions (M x+ = Na+, K+, Ag+, Ca2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Cr3+, Al3+ and Fe3+, respectively). Further, the mixed solutions were treated via ultrasonication for 30 min, and used for the measurements of fluorescence recognition. The equation (1) is applied to calculate the fluorescence quenching efficiency (Q). Moreover, the Stern‒Volmer equation (2) is employed to estimate the process of fluorescence quenching.
(1)
Q =
[
(
F
0
− F
)
/
F
0
]
× 100 %
(2)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K
s v
[ M ]
wherein F 0 and F are the fluorescence emission intensity without and with analyte, respectively; K sv is the quenching constant; [M] represents the concentration of analyte.

NCST-3 crystallizes in the monoclinic system with P2
1
/n space group. The asymmetric unit consists of one crystallographically independent Mn(II) center, one L ligand, one NDC2− linker, and one coordinated water molecule. As illustrated in Fig. 1 a, the Mn(II) ion is surrounded by three oxygen atoms (O1, O4A and O3B) from three carboxylic groups within distinct NDC2−, one oxygen (O1W) of coordinated water, and two nitrogen atoms (N1 and N4C) from two benzimidazole rings of different L ligands, resulting into a distorted {MnO4N2} octahedral geometry (symmetry code: A = x, y-1, z; B = -x, y-1, -z+1; C = x-0.5, -y+0.5, z+0.5.). Additionally, the lengths of MnO bond are in the range of 2.168(3) – 2.460(3) Å, whereas the MnN bond length is 2.301(3) Å. Comparing with previously reported Mn(II) coordination polymers based on similar bis(benzimidazole), the bond lengths of MnO and MnN are in the normal range [18].

Along the c axis, each L, as μ 2 ligand, employes trans-conformation to joint neighbouring Mn(II) centers, forming a 1D wave-like [Mn(L)] n chain with the Mn⋯Mn separation distance of 12.682(6) Å (Fig. S1a). Meanwhile, The completely deprotonated NDC2− ligands are involved in (κ 1-κ 1)-(κ 1-κ 0)-μ 3 coordination bridging contiguous Mn(II) ions to construct a 1D ladder [Mn(NDC)] n chains with the Mn⋯Mn distance of 11.613(2) Å and 4.857(4) Å along the b axis, which includes a special hexatomic ring generated by two Mn(II) ions, and four oxygen atoms form different NDC2− ligands (Fig. S1b), generating a two dimensional network in the bc plane as depicted in Fig. 1b. Topologically, the 2D network is described as a binodal (3,5)-connected 3,5L2 network with the point symbol of (42.67.8)(42.6) by the ToposPro program (Fig. 1c). Furthermore, the 2D net is expanded into a 3D supramolecular architecture via CH···π stacking interactions by C33H33B and benzene rings (Cg1: C14C15C16C19C20–21) with the H33B···Cg1 distance of 2.817(3) Å and ∠C(33)–H(33)B···Cg1 = 148.03(12)° (Fig. 1d).

NCST-4 crystallizes in the monoclinic space group P2(1), and its asymmetric unit contains two Mn(Ⅱ) atoms, two L, two HBTC− ligands, two coordinated water molecules, and two lattice water molecules. As described in Fig. 2 a, coordination environments of the Mn(1) and the Mn(2) are similar, two metal centers are coordinated by six atoms to form different distorted octahedral geometries. As for Mn(1), four oxygen atoms (O1, O5A, O1W and O2W) of four vertices within {MnO4N2} octahedral geometry are from two HBTC−, and two coordinated waters, respectively, and two nitrogen atoms (N4B and N8C) come from different L ligands (symmetry code: A = x, y-1, z; B = -x, y-0.5, -z; C = -x+1, y-0.5, -z+1.). Meanwhile, the Mn(2) is surrounded by four oxygen atoms (O2, O9, O11A and O12A) from three diverse HBTC−, and two nitrogen atoms (N1 and N5) from disparate L ligands. The bond lengths of MnO and MnN, from 2.151(4) to 2.501(5) Å, are all normal [33].

L ligands with trans-conformation bridged Mn(II) centers to generate a 1D linear [Mn(L)] n chains with Mn(1)···Mn(2) = 11.604(2) Å along the c axis (Fig. S2a). Simultaneously, the HBTC− ligands adopt (κ 1-κ 1)-(κ 1-κ 0)-(κ 0-κ 0)-μ 2 and (κ 1-κ 1)-(κ 1-κ 0)-(κ 0-κ 0)-μ 3 coordination modes to link Mn(II) ions, forming a 1D ladder [Mn(HBTC)] n chains along the b axis (Fig. S2b). The Mn⋯Mn separation distances within the chain bridged by the HBTC− ligand are 5.443(1) and 10.216(2) Å. Consequently, a 2D coordination network is formed in the bc plane (Fig. 2b). It can be simplified into a rare (3,4,5)-connected framework and point symbol with a (53)(55.6)(56.62.82) (Fig. 2c). Additionally, it is further extended into a 3D supramolecular framework through hydrogen bond interactions [O13⋯O6 = 2.625(7) Å and ∠O(13)H(13)···O6 = 165.98(8)° ] (Fig. 2d).

As described in Fig. S3a, FT-IR spectra of NCST-3 and NCST-4 exhibit strong and broad stretching absorption band around 3400 cm−1, which is owing to the telescopic vibration of OH group within coordinated water. For NCST-3, NDC2− ligands are all completely deprotonated because there is no obvious band around 1700 cm−1. While the quintessential band in 1704 cm−1 for NCST-4 reveals that the HBTC2− ligands are not completely deprotonated. Distinguishable differences in anti-symmetric and symmetric stretching vibration of carboxylate group are observed for two coordination polymers. The strong intensities found at 1573, 1406 and 1363 cm−1 for NCST-3 and the values [ν as(COO) – ν s(COO)] = 167 cm−1 and 210 cm−1, implying that each NDC2− in NCST-3 adopts bidentate bridging, and monodentate coordination modes. The peaks of 1613, 1562, 1445 and 1376 cm−1 in NCST-4 are observed. Furthermore, three types of Δν values (Δν = ν as(COO) – ν s(COO)) are less than 150 cm−1, in the range of 150–200 cm−1, and more than 200 cm−1, respectively, indicating the presence of chelating, bidentate bridging, and monodentate carboxylic groups within NCST-4. The peak around 1500 cm−1 found in the both of NCST-3 (1508 cm−1) and NCST-4 (1506 cm−1) is attributed to the CN stretching vibrations of benzimidazole within L [34].

As illustrated in Fig. S3b (Left), the thermogravimetric analyses (TGA) revealed that a mass loss of 2.69% (calc. 2.84%) appeared from 96 to 136 °C, which is due to the release of a coordinated H2O molecule in NCST-3. A long platform period was observed in the region of 136–262 °C. Then the weight loss from 262 to 484 °C (obs. 53.57%; calc. 54.68%) is ascribed to the decomposition of L ligands. When the temperature is higher than the turning point (484 °C), the network of NCST-3 further collapsed rapidly with the mass loss of 32.83% (calc. 31.28%) until to 519 °C, owing to the fact that NDC2− ligands are disintegrated from the coordination polymer. Consequently, the remaining product should be MnO (obs. 10.91%; calc. 11.20%).

Fig. S3b (Right) described the thermal stability of NCST-4. From 51 to 158 °C, the weight loss of 5.44% (calc. 5.58%) is due to the evaporation of two coordinated and two free water molecules in NCST-4. The mass loss (obs. 52.49%; calc. 53.59%), after a long stable stage (158–329 °C), was observed in the temperature range of 329–422 °C, which is attributed to the lost L ligands within NCST-4. Then HBTC2− ligands (obs. 30.11%; calc. 29.14%) were decomposed from this coordination polymer when the temperature rises from 422 °C to 582 °C. Finally, the weight of remaining substance is 11.54% (calc. 10.99%), and it is inferred to MnO.

Moreover, the powder X-ray diffraction patterns (PXRD) of NCST-3 and NCST-4 were highly consistent with that obtained from CIF of single-crystal data (Fig. S4), resulting the pure phases of these two powders.

UV–vis adsorption spectra of the free solid-state L, 1,4-H2NDC, and H3BTC ligands, NCST-3 and NCST-4 were collected by Puxi T9 UV–vis spectrophotometer. According to the Kubelka–Munk (K–M) function FR = (1–R)2/2R (R is the diffuse reflectance of the substance), the band-gap energy is 3.37 eV for NCST-3, and 3.92 eV for NCST-4, respectively (Fig. S5). Further, the photocatalytic activities of two CPs were evaluated by degrading four organic dyes including methylene blue (MB), Rhodamine B (RhB), methyl violet (MV) and methyl orange (MO) under the UV irradiation. After 2 h, efficiencies of NCST-3 reached up to 98.13% for MB, and 98.24% for RhB, however, the degradation efficiencies are 88.55% for MV, and 69.74% for MO after 2.5 h. As for NCST-4, efficiencies towards different dyes are 89.93%, 97.70%, 82.95%, and 21.13% for MB, RhB, MV and MO, respectively (Fig. S6). Detailed information is provided in the Supplementary material.

To investigate the fluorescent properties, the emission spectra of NCST-3, NCST-4 and ligand L were detected in the solid state (Fig. S7). The maximum emission intensity of L was observed at 312 nm (λex = 279 nm), being assigned to the electronic transitions of π*→π or π*→n [35]. The maximum peaks were at 376 nm (λex = 313 nm) for NCST-3, and 393 nm (λex = 296 nm) for NCST-4, respectively. Comparing with ligand L, the red-shifted phenomenon was found in NCST-3 (Δ = 64 nm) and NCST-4 (Δ = 81 nm), owing to the ligand-to-metal charge-transfer (LMCT) transition [36,37].

In order to the exploration of different solvents for emission intensity, 4 mg of powder were suspended in 15 different solvents of 4 mL such as H2O, methanol (MeOH), ethanol (EtOH), ethylene glycol (EG), iso-propanol (IPA), n-butanol (NBA), acetone (DMK), butanone (MEK), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), N,N-dimethylacetamide (DMAC), N-methyl pyrrolidone (NMP), dichloromethane (DCM), acetonitrile (CH3CN), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), cyclohexane (CYH). The results prove that the fluorescent emission intensities of NCST-3 suspended in different small molecule solvents follow the order: EtOH > NBA > CH3CN > DMF > IPA > DMAC > NMP > MEK > MeOH > DMK > EG > DCM > CYH > H2O > DMSO (Fig. 3 a). Nevertheless, the emission intensities of NCST-4 reduced in the order of DMAC > NMP > EG > DCM > DMF > IPA > CYH > NBA > EtOH > CH3CN > MeOH > H2O > MEK > DMK > DMSO (Fig. 3b). These results revealed that emission intensities of NCST-3 and NCST-4 were intensely affected by different solvents through fluorescence quenching or enhancement effect. After immersing in these disparate solvents for 24 h, the PXRD patterns of NCST-3 and NCST-4 highly match with that of the as-synthesized samples, demonstrating that the two coordination polymers display high resistance and stability against a wide range of solvents (Fig. S8).

To gain further insight into the identification of different ions by NCST-3, 4 mg of powder were suspended in 4 mL EtOH solvent and added into different kinds of ions (the cations viz Na+, K+, Ag+, Ca2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Cr3+, Al3+, and Fe3+). When the probe is NCST-4, the experimental procedure is the same as above except that the solvent is replaced by DMAC. As depicted in Fig. 4 a, NCST-3 reveals different fluorescence quenching effect for these ions except that Al3+ slightly enhances the emission intensity. The intensity of NCST-4 sharply decreased by the addition of Ag+, while other cations exhibit influence of different degrees on it (Fig. 4b). Further, from the equation (1), the order of quenching efficiencies are Cr3+ (97.93%) > Fe3+ (94.06%) > Cu2+ (93.61%) > Pb2+ (77.99%) > Ni2+ (74.69%) > Ca2+ (69.43%) > Co2+ (65.85%) > Mg2+ (63.54%) > Ag+ (60.96%) > Cd2+ (52.22%) > Zn2+ (46.02%) > Na+ (11.85%) > Ba2+ (9.65%) > Mn2+ (7.56%) > Hg2+ (2.61%) > K+ (2.10%) > Al3+ (−6.31%) for NCST-3, and Ag+ (97.99%) > Fe3+ (95.69%) > Cu2+ (93.09%) > Cr3+ (73.88%) > Co2+ (47.80%) > Ni2+ (22.64%) > Mn2+ (16.54%) > Pb2+ (10.37%) > Hg2+ (8.86%) > Ca2+ (8.20%) > Na+ (3.81%) > Ba2+ (3.56%) > K+ (2.93%) > Cd2+ (2.84%) > Mg2+ (−0.77%) > Zn2+ (−4.30%) > Al3+ (−26.05%) for NCST-4, respectively.

For further understanding the fluorescence response of NCST-3 toward Cr3+, the concentration-fluorescence intensity measurements were carried out. 4 mg powder was suspended in 4 mL EtOH with different concentrations (0–100 μM) of Cr(NO3)3, and the emission intensities were investigated (Fig. 5 a). According to the equation (2), the graph of F 0/F versus the concentration of Cr3+ was described in Fig. 5b, and the data was fitted to a straight line with a vertical intercept of 1 at low concentrations (0–50 μM). The fitted equation is F 0/F = 1 + 1.31 × 104 [Cr3+] (R2 = 0.9997), additionally, the Stern‒Volmer quenching constant Ksv is 1.31 × 104 M−1 in the quenching experiment towards Cr3+. Moreover, based on these, we could estimate the limit of detection (LOD) by the formula of 3σ/k (σ: standard, k: slope). The LOD value of 0.25 μM (S/N = 3) is calculated. However, the graphs of F 0/F versus the concentration exhibit a non-linear when the concentration is higher than a certain point, demonstrating the coexistence of simultaneous static and dynamic quenching in this process [38]. When NCST-4 serves as for Ag+, the relationship curve can be fitted by the Stern‒Volmer equation F 0/F = 1 + 9.72 × 103 [Ag+] (R2 = 0.9987), and LOD = 0.67 μM (S/N = 3) in the region of 0–100 μM (Fig. 6 ). Comparing with other reported fluorescence sensors for detecting Cr3+ and Ag+, NCST-3 and NCST-4 can be regarded as probes with lower LOD (Table 3 ).

To examine the influence of other metal ions, the competitive experiments were implemented in the presence of other ions. Cr(NO3)3 (0.02 mmol) and M(NO3) x of 0.02 mmol (M x+ = Na+, K+, Ag+, Ca2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Al3+, and Fe3+, respectively.) was put into 4 mL EtOH containing 4 mg NCST-3. The selectivity of NCST-4 to Ag+ was validated by the above similar process. There is no obvious influence on NCST-3 and NCST-4 in the presence of other ions (Fig. 7 ). Additionally, the quenching percentages and fluorescence emission intensities of NCST-3 and NCST-4 were almost not changed after five consecutive cycles (Fig. S9). These results demonstrate that the two coordination polymers as fluorescent sensing materials possess higher anti-interference performance and recyclability.

The influence of reaction time between the two probes and ions (NCST-3 for Cr3+, NCST-4 for Ag+.) were investigated (Fig. S10a). The recognition interactions of fluorescent materials to ions were accomplished immediately. When NCST-3 recognized Cr3+ ion by fluorescence quenching, the emission intensity of it reduced to the minimum value at 1 min, and remained nearly constant after that. Nevertheless, when NCST-4 as probe detects Ag+, the reaction time was only 2 min, (Fig. S10b). As a consequence, the fluorescent sensors of NCST-3 and NCST-4 could be applied in real-time tracking of certain ions (Cr3+, Ag+) in organism.

To further estimate the sensing mechanism of coordination polymers towards some ions, the UV–vis absorption spectra of NCST-3 in EtOH without and with Cr3+ were collected at the ambient temperature. In DMAC, these of NCST-4 were measured in the presence/absence of Ag+ ion. No great change was observed at the strong absorption peak, certifying that there is no chemical reaction between ions and coordination polymers in the quenching process (Fig. S11a). Furthermore, there was no obvious change of quenching percentages and emission intensities over five cycles, proving the reusability and the stability of NCST-3 and NCST-4 (Fig. S9). The PXRD patterns of two materials after detecting ions for five cycles were highly coincident with the simulated pattern (Fig. S11b), indicating that the frameworks of NCST-3 and NCST-4 are stable and there is no collapse phenomenon after testing these ions. Finally, the UV–vis absorption spectra of the M(NO3) x in EtOH solution were measured as well as in DMAC (Fig. S12). There are two overlaps: one between Cr3+ and excitation of NCST-3 in the range of 305–324 nm, and the other between Ag+ and excitation of NCST-4 from 298 to 328 nm. Therefore, the most significant factor of selective ion-sensing is not the interaction or ion exchange of metal center and targeted ions, not the complex formation between ions and coordination compound, not the collapse of the framework, may be as competitive absorption of energy of excitation wavelength between ions and these materials.

Two ternary luminescence Mn(II) CPs were successfully synthesized with bis(benzimidazole) and carboxylates ligands, which feature distinct 2D networks. Two complexes as a fluorescence probes display highly sensitivity and selectivity for the detection of Cr3+/Ag+ with large Stern‒Volmer quenching constant (Ksv). NCST-3 presents higher photocatalytic activities than NCST-4 for the degradation of four organic dyes under UV irradiation.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The project was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51474086), Natural Science Foundation-Steel and Iron Foundation of Hebei Province (B2015209299).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

CCDC 1857119 and 1840396 contain the supplementary crystallographic data for NCST-3 and NCST-4. These data can be obtained free of charge via http://www.ccdc.cam.ac.uk/conts/retrieving.html, or from the Cambridge Crystallographic Data Centre, 12 Union Road, Cambridge CB2 1EZ, UK; fax: (+44) 1223-336-033; or e-mail: deposit@ccdc.cam.ac.uk.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jssc.2019.02.027.
